title: Proper Nouns in Harakah Rules: Non-Arabic Names and Exceptions
summary: The session focuses on applying harakah rules to proper nouns, particularly non-Arabic names. The teacher explains exceptions where words do not follow the standard kasrah rule due to being ghayr munsarif (non-prolongable), emphasizing examples like Musajid, Washington, and London.
key_points: Non-Arabic names (e.g., Londnًا) use fathah instead of kasrah because they are considered ghَيْر مُنْصَرِف (non-prolongable).
Words in specific patterns like مَفْعَل (e.g., مساجد, Masajid) or proper nouns with tamarboo’ah (ة) retain fathah: مروان (Marwān), not Marwānin.
Exceptions include names ending with 'an' such as Makkah (مَكَّة) which take fathah despite being feminine. Musar (school) requires kasrah due to grammatical context.
questions: [00:02:00] Q: Why does Harun get a fatha instead of kasra? — A: Because it's a non-Arabic name, thus an exception (ghayr munsarif).
[00:04:00] Q: Why is Makkah مakkah and not Makkatin? — A: It's a female city name, so fatha applies despite the rule.
[00:09:06] Q: Why is Masajid used with fathah in Min Musajidatun? — A: Due to being non-Arabic and ghayr munsarif.
